title: The Impact of AI on Developer Productivity: Evidence from GitHub Copilot
study_design_description: A controlled randomized experiment was conducted from May 15, 2022 to June 20, 2022 to measure the productivity impact of GitHub Copilot. Ninety-five professional programmers recruited through Upwork were randomly assigned to control and treatment groups (45 treated, 50 control), with 35 completing both the task and survey. The experiment used GitHub Classroom to administer a standardized task and precisely measure timing and completion.
primary_task_description: Recruited software developers were asked to implement an HTTP server in JavaScript as quickly as possible. The treatment group used GitHub Copilot to complete the task, while the control group did not. Participants received a personal copy of a template repository with task description and skeleton codebase, along with a test suite comprising twelve checks for submission correctness. Participants could use any sources of information (internet search, Stack Overflow) as they normally do. Task completion time was measured from repository creation to the first commit that successfully passed all 12 tests.
population_description: A total of 95 developers were recruited through Upwork and randomly assigned into control and treated groups. Most participants were in the age group of 25-34 and came from India and Pakistan, characterized by relatively lower income (median yearly income between $10,000-$19,000) but high education level (majority with a 4-year degree and above). The group had an average coding experience of 6 years and reported spending 9 hours on coding in a working day on average.

arms.description: The treated group was instructed to watch a 1-minute video introducing them to GitHub Copilot. They received an automated email with installation instructions for GitHub Copilot once granted access to the tool. Telemetry verified that all participants from the treated group configured GitHub Copilot and accepted recommendations other than five who did not finish the sign up and thus started the experiment without the GitHub Copilot.
The control group did not have access to GitHub Copilot but was otherwise unconstrained, i.e., they were free to use internet search and Stack Overflow to complete the task.

outcomes.outcome_description: Task completion time was measured as the time from start to end of the task. The time elapsed between the timestamp of repository creation and the timestamp of the first commit to successfully pass all 12 tests was counted as the participant's task completion time.
Task success was measured as the percentage of participants in a group that adequately completed the task.
Participants in both treated and control groups estimated their productivity increase.
Average irrelevant price (willingness to pay) for GitHub Copilot per month.

reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: The treatment group, with access to the AI pair programmer, completed the task 55.8% faster than the control group. ... the treated group completed the task 55.8% faster (95% confidence interval: 21-89%). ... The p-value for the t-test is 0.0017, and a 95% confidence interval for the improvement is between [21%, 89%].

Conditioning on completing the task, the average completion time from the treated group is 71.17 minutes and 160.89 minutes for the control group. This represents a 55.8% reduction in completion time.

We also find that the treated group's success rate is 7 percentage points higher than the control group, but the estimate is not statistically significant, with a 95% confidence interval of [-0.11, 0.25].
Programming experience (years): Estimate 8.23, SE 4.36, t-Stat 1.90, p-Value 0.0629

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: heterogeneous effect: hours of programming per day
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Hours of programming per day: Estimate -11.70, SE 4.74, t-Stat -2.47, p-Value 0.0168
Income less than $20,000: Estimate 0.64, SE 27.47, t-Stat 0.02, p-Value 0.9814
No college: Estimate -36.57, SE 32.89, t-Stat -1.11, p-Value 0.2711
Language Preference: Java: Estimate -11.77, SE 33.16, t-Stat -0.35, p-Value 0.7240
Language Preference: Python: Estimate 22.90, SE 42.19, t-Stat 0.54, p-Value 0.5895
On average, participants in both treated and control groups estimated a 35% increase in productivity, which is an underestimation compared with the 55.8% increase in their revealed productivity.

The average irrelevant price for the treated group is $27.25, and the average irrelevant price for the control group is $16.91, both per month. The difference is statistically significant at the 95% level.

notes: Study examined a standardized programming task in a controlled experiment rather than real-world collaborative development. Productivity benefits may vary across specific tasks and programming languages. The study does not examine effects of AI on code quality. Five participants in the treated group did not complete GitHub Copilot setup and started the experiment without the tool. Four outliers with completion time above 300 minutes were all in the control group; results remain robust if outliers are dropped. Thirty-five of 95 recruited developers completed both task and survey. Self-reported productivity gains (35%) were lower than revealed productivity gains (55.8%).
